content.full_text:
Farn Por-rrrcAL PnecrrcBs CoMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 9581.1-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) -122-0886
Jtrly 29,2010
Gillian Calof
Operations Director
Climate Action Reserve
523 W.6th Street, Suite 428
Los Angeles, CA 90014
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-10-107
Dear Ms. Calof:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").' This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair political
Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is general in nature, we are treating youi
request as one for informal assistance.
QUESTION
Would the Climate Action Reserve (CAR) be the solrrces of gifts conferred on public
officials when CAR pays for public officials to travel nationally andlnternationally onirips
related to climate policy where the trip is funded by donations from corporations and businesses
with matters before state elected officials and state agencies?
CONCLUSION
Under your facts, the reportable source of the gifts to nonpaying public officials would be
the sponsors who donated money to CAR, unless their rnoney was segregated and not used for
sr'rch purposes. Thus, the benefit of the gift received by the official would be pro-rated among
the donors that funded the gift. Each donor would be subject ro the $420 gift iimit and would be
reportable as a source of the gift on the official's statement of economic interests at $50.
I The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections tt l(X)0 through 9 1014. All statutory
ret'erences are to the Covernment Code, unless otherwise indicatetl. The regulations of the f'air political practices
Conrmission are contained in Sections l 8 l l0 throu-qh l 8997 of Title 2 of the Calittrrnia Co<te of Regulations. All
regulatory rcf'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 olthe Califbrnia Code of Regulatigns, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. I-10-107
Page No. 2
FACTS2
You stated that CAR is registered as a nonprofit 501(cX3) organization with a mission to
ensLlre integrity, transparency, and financial value in the North American carbon market. CAR is
a registry for greenhouse gas emissions reduction projects in North America, issuing carbon
offset credits generated from such projects and tract<ing the transaction of credits over time in a
transparent, publicly-accessible system.
In furtherance of your mission, you host delegations on educational study trips regarding
national and international climate policy, including the 2009 united Nations Climate change
Conference in Copenhagen, Denmark. {1host, you arrange and pay for travel, lodging and
events for the delegates, who include staff, corporate partn-ers, and in some cases state elected
officials and government agency staff.
You ftrnd these study trips through ftrndraising campaigns and donations from your
members, foundations, stakeholders, and unrestrictedhnds of the CAR. Donors include
corporations and businesses with matters before state elected officials and state agencies. you
state that trips are usually financed by between 5 and 20 sponsors. The sponsors are
acknowledged in written materials by includin-g sponsor logos and/or thanking them as sponsors.
Sponsor payments are tax deductable less the fair-market vilue of any goods ind services
received. This is calculated for each sponsoring organization dependinlg on the number of
individuals and their costs.
For example, in connection with the delegation sent to the United Nations Climate
change conference 2009, cAR solicited three sponsorship levels:
1. Stellar Sponsor - $50,000
o official participation as an observer delegate for three company representatives
o Logistical and planning sttpport, including three guaranteed hotel room reservationsf
o Panel speaking opportunity at side event (by mutuar agreement)
t Opportunity to deliver welcome remarks at delegation event (by mutual agreement)
o Signature sponsorship of a delegation luncheon or dinner event (by mutual agreement)
o Three seats to wednesday evening exclusive vIP dinner for delegation with international
leaders
o Three tickets to the gala reception on December lS,2OO9
o Sponsorship recognition at gala reception
o Prominent corporate logo position on all delegation materials, side events and web site
o Facilitation of meetings and interviews with other delegates, organizations, and media
o Participation in daily breakfast briefings
o Summary of proceedings and briefing notes
20t0.
r You clarit'ied the tacts in your letter requesting advice in three emails dated June 16, 22, and July 12.


File No. I-10-107
Page No. 3
o Up to three individuals may be added to the delegation at an additional cost of $2,000 per
person (so long as the name and passport number were received by November 6,2009)
2. Visionary Sponsor - $25,000
o Official participation as an observer delegate for two company representatives
o Logistical and planning sttpport, including two gllaranteed hotel room reservationsf
o Panel speaking opportunity at side event (by mutual agreement)
. 
Sponsorship recognition at gala reception
o Two seats at Wednesday evening exclusive VIp dinner
o Two invitations to gala reception on December 15, 2009
o Recognition in delegation materials
o Participation in daily breakfast briefings
o Summary of proceedings and briefing notes
3. Leader Sponsor - $10,000
o official participation as an observer at the Bella Center for one company representative
o Logistical and planning support, including one guaranteed hotel ,oo* r.r"rvationt
o Participation in daily breakfast briefings
. 
Opening and closing delegation dinners
o Recognition in printed materials
o Invitation to gala reception
These sponsorship payments did not include travel costs.
- 
The sponsors are aware that their donations fund the costs associated with the attendance
of state elected officials and agency staff, though they are not aware of which officials they are
ftrnding. rn2009, for example, of the 13 delegates that received free ,;r;;;;;ion from cAR,
nine were agency representatives/public officials.
You also statedthat the sponsors do not direct you in any way in choosing which elected
officials or agency staff should be invited on the study trips or how their donations should be
spent' Moreover, you stated that CAR never shares tire list of delegates with confirmed sponsors
until a week or two before the event, at which point solicitation foinew sponsors would have
ended' Potential sponsors may have a sense of who attended in the past, but would not be privy
to any names during your fundraising efforts.


File No. I-10-107
Page No. 4
ANALYSIS
Making and Receipt of Gifts
The term "gifr" is defined in Section g202g(a) as:
"AnY payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that
consideration ofequal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is-made in
the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official
status.'"
The Act regulates the receipt of gifts by local public officials in three ways rhat are
relevant to your question.
o First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials.
Certain state and local officials are prohibitid from accepting gifts from any single source
in any calendar year with a total value of more than the gift tliit. lSe.tion g950i3(a).)
The current gift limit, which is adjusted biennially each idd numbei year to reflect
changes in the Consumer price Index, is $420. (Regulation lgg4}.2.\
o Secondly, so that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the Act
imposes reporting obligations requiring certain puUti. officials to disclose the source of
any gifts aggregating $50 or more in value. SeCtion 87203 provides that "[e.lvery person
who holds an office specified in Section 87200 shall . . . fil; a statemenr disclosing his [or
her] investments, his [or her] interests in real property and his [or her] income . . ..,, The
term "income" includes any gift. (Section g2030-.)
' 
Finally, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
using his or her position to influence the outcome of u go-uon*ental dJcision invliving
the donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value or $+zo or more provided to, received
by, or promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made.
(Sections 87100, 87103(e), Regulations 1g700, 1g703.4.)
Notwithstanding the general gift provisions explained above, Section g9506 controls
whether a gift of travel is subject to the Act's gift limii, providing the following in pertinent part:
"(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that or"."oronobly related to a
legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or
international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if either of
the following apply:
I Section 82044 detrnes payment. in part, as any ,.rendering of
whether tangible or intangible."
services or anything else of value,


File No. I-10-107
Page No. 5
'k**
"(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a foreign
government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private educational
institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation Code, a
nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the
Internal Revenue Code, or a by a person domiciled outside the United States
which substantially satisfies the requirements for tax exempt status under Section
501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code."
Based upon the facts you have provided, the travel in question appears to be reasonably
related to an issue of state, national, or international public pollcy in ttrai ihe travel will give
officials information on climate policy. If the source of any payment for the travel is CAR,
which is a non-profit organization exempt from taxation unO", 
-Section 
501(c)(3) of the Intemal
Revenue Code, the payment is not subject to the Act's $420 gift limit.a rr trre true soLlrce of the
gifts is not one of the entities set forth in Section 89506(a)(2), the gift limit would apply.
Source of Gift
Notwithstanding,-the gift exception for travel payments set forth above, you have
indicated that payments for the "educational study triis'; will come from CAR,s donors and
sponsors. Thus, you must determine whether the souice of any particular payment is CAR or the
donor or sponsor making a payment to CAR for the event. Retently aooptei Regulation 1g945
provides the Commission's rule for determining the source of a gifiand provides the following,
in pertinent part:
"(a) General Rule. A person is the source of a gift if the person makes a gift to an
official and is not acting as an intermediary.
"(b) Gifts through an Intermediary.
"(1) A person is the source of a gift, and a third party is an intermediary of
the gift under Sections 87210 or 87313, if the person makes a payment to the third
party and the payment is used directly or indirectly by the third party to make a
gift to an official under any of the foilowing conditions:
"(A) The person directs and controls the payment at the time it is used by
the third party to make a gift to the official
4 while a payment for travel by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization is not subject to the $420 gift limit, any
payment must be disclosed as a reportable gifi under Sectlon 87203. Moreover, the payment may uar the public
official who received the payment tiom making, participating in making, or inf'luencing gor..n*.nt decisions
affecting CAR. if they Y:.:. th: source of the git ru.n ir a lirt of travll from a sor rc"x5l nonfrotit organization is
exempt from the $420 gift limit. the official must srill disquai-ify himself or herself t'.,n'r;t g;;ernmenral decision
with a reasonably tbreseeable material financial ef'fect on ih. ,,ru... of a -eifi or gifis wittr an iggr.gate value of
$420 or more in the l2-month period prior to his or her participation in the government decision. (Section g7 l0o et
seq.)


File No. I-10-107
Page No. 6
"(B) The person and third party have agreed that the payment will be Lrsed
by the third parry to make a gift to the official.
"(C) The person identifies the official to the third parry as the intended
beneficiary of the payment prior to the third party making thcpayment to the
official.
"(D) The third party identifies the official to the person as the intended
beneficiary of the payment prior to the person making the payment to the third
party.
"(E) The person knows or has reason to know that the sole or primary
purpose of the payment is to make gifts to officials.
"(F) The official or the official's agent solicits the payment from the
person to the third party for the pLlrpose of making a gift to the official.,,
Based on ygur facts, the only provision that seems to apply to your educational study
trips would be subdivision (E). Subdivision (E) focuses on the;rtt. * primary,'purpose for
making a payment, necessarily based on the objective facts and circumsian.er it ut "rid"n"" 
an
intent of the donor. [n footnote 3 of the Commission Memorandum concerning the adoption of
Regulation 18945 (Amendment to Regulation 18945 - Source of Gifts, March77, 2010j, staff
provided the following continuum:
"For example, a person may make a payment to a third person specifically for an
identified conference knowing all attendees will be state legislators and that the
payment will ultimately pay for the legislators' travel and accommodations. [n
these circumstances, the person making the payment to the third party should be
considered the source of the gifts.
tltl
"On the other hand, if a person makes a payment for a widely attended event,
there is little indication that the payment will be used to makl a gift to an official
even when the person knows or has reason to know that the official will attend the
event. [n these circumstances, the person making the payment to the third party
should not be considered the source of a gift providedlo the official from the third
party hosting the event."
Your situation is more closely associated with the first example. You noted that the
donors are aware that their contributions, over and above their own "ortr, will ttnd the costs
associated with the attendance of nonpaying delegates, including state elected officials and
agency stafT. You also noted that historically the majority of delegates receiving the free trips
were public officials. In other words, the sponsors know, or haveieason to knoiv. that their


File No. I-10-107
Page No. 7
payments will fund free attendance for a group of persons, a substantial number of which are
public officials.
These factors sLlggest that the primary pllrpose of the payments is to make gifts to public
officials generally and if the payments are ultimatily used foithat plrrpose, the paymenrs are
gifts from the sponsors. Therefore, under your facts, the source ofihagifts to the officials are
the sponsors to CAR, not CAR itself.
If yotr have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (gL6) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
c-
John W. Wallace
Jwwjgl
',Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
B

embedding.first_500_words: Farn Por-rrrcAL PnecrrcBs CoMMrssroN 428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 9581.1-2329 (916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) -122-0886 Jtrly 29,2010 Gillian Calof Operations Director Climate Action Reserve 523 W.6th Street, Suite 428 Los Angeles, CA 90014 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-10-107 Dear Ms. Calof: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is general in nature, we are treating youi request as one for informal assistance. QUESTION Would the Climate Action Reserve (CAR) be the solrrces of gifts conferred on public officials when CAR pays for public officials to travel nationally andlnternationally onirips related to climate policy where the trip is funded by donations from corporations and businesses with matters before state elected officials and state agencies? CONCLUSION Under your facts, the reportable source of the gifts to nonpaying public officials would be the sponsors who donated money to CAR, unless their rnoney was segregated and not used for sr'rch purposes. Thus, the benefit of the gift received by the official would be pro-rated among the donors that funded the gift. Each donor would be subject ro the $420 gift iimit and would be reportable as a source of the gift on the official's statement of economic interests at $50. I The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections tt l(X)0 through 9 1014. All statutory ret'erences are to the Covernment Code, unless otherwise indicatetl. The regulations of the f'air political practices Conrmission are contained in Sections l 8 l l0 throu-qh l 8997 of Title 2 of the Calittrrnia Co<te of Regulations. All regulatory rcf'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 olthe Califbrnia Code of Regulatigns, unless otherwise indicated. File No. I-10-107 Page No. 2 FACTS2 You stated that CAR is registered as a nonprofit 501(cX3) organization with a mission to ensLlre integrity, transparency, and financial value in the North American carbon market. CAR is a registry for greenhouse gas emissions reduction projects in North America, issuing carbon offset credits generated from such projects and tract<ing the transaction of credits over time in a transparent, publicly-accessible system. In furtherance of your mission, you host delegations on educational study trips regarding national and international climate policy, including the 2009 united Nations Climate change Conference in Copenhagen, Denmark. {1host, you arrange and pay for travel, lodging and events for the delegates, who include staff, corporate partn-ers, and in some cases state elected officials and government agency staff. You ftrnd these study trips through ftrndraising campaigns and donations from your members, foundations, stakeholders, and unrestrictedhnds of the CAR.
